content:
3 min read
Just now

--

Back in March, I hooked up an MCP server so Claude Code could read my project files. It took 10 minutes. It worked perfectly. Then I checked what else it could see and realized: this thing has access to everything on my machine.
That’s the MCP security problem in one sentence. The protocol was designed for convenience. Security was, at best, an afterthought.
I’ve been building with MCP for a few months now — servers for file access, GitHub, PostgreSQL, even one that controls my browser. The developer experience is genuinely great. But the more I dug into the security side, the more worried I got.
The core problem
MCP servers use stdio transport. That means they communicate through standard input/output pipes — no network boundary, no sandbox, no isolation. A weather server and a file system server both run with the same privileges as your terminal.
The protocol has a capabilities system: tools, resources, prompts. But capabilities aren’t access controls. A file server that can read your SSH keys declares the same capability type as a weather server that fetches forecasts. There’s no way to say “this tool can read but not write.”
The numbers are worse than you think
As of June 2026, there are 30+ CVEs across various MCP implementations. The one that keeps me up at night is a CVSS 9.6 — remote code execution through a malicious MCP server.
The vulnerability chain is simple:

• Client connects to a server

• Client calls tools/list to discover available tools

• Server returns tool definitions

• Server sends back a response with embedded commands

• Client doesn’t validate the output — it just executes
Some clients have added validation since the CVE was published. But the protocol itself doesn’t require it.
Three gaps that worry me
No code review in the registry
The MCP registry lets anyone publish a server. There’s no malware scanning, no code review, no equivalent of npm audit. Compare this to the Chrome Web Store or the VS Code marketplace — both have review processes. MCP has nothing.
No least privilege
You either trust a server completely or you don’t use it. There’s no granular permission model. I can’t tell my MCP client “this server can read files in /projects but not in /Users/me/.ssh.” It’s all or nothing.
Auto-discovery with no approval
When an MCP client connects to a server, it immediately fetches the full tool list. In most clients, tools are callable without any per-tool approval. A server that exposes delete_everything and read_all_files looks identical to a server with read_config and search_docs in the capabilities handshake.
What I changed
After this research, I made three changes to my setup:

• Docker isolation. Every MCP server runs in its own container. No host filesystem access unless I explicitly mount a volume. If a server goes rogue, it can’t touch my SSH keys or my browser history.

• Manual audit. Before installing a new server, I read the source code. If there’s no source, I don’t install it.

• Review every call. Claude Code shows me every tool call before executing. I used to auto-approve. I don’t anymore.
Things I still haven’t figured out
I think MCP needs a security redesign, but I’m not sure what the right answer looks like. A few open questions:

• Should MCP servers run in a WASM sandbox with no native filesystem access?

• Should the protocol include a permission manifest — a machine-readable file that declares exactly what resources a server needs?

• Who should own access control — the protocol layer, the client, or the server itself?

• How do you make security visible without ruining the UX? If every tool call pops a confirmation dialog, developers will just click “allow all.”
Bottom line
MCP is genuinely useful. I’ll keep using it and building with it. But treating it like just another API protocol is a mistake. It’s a local execution environment. You should think about it the same way you’d think about running untrusted code on your machine.
If you’re running MCP servers, take 10 minutes today to audit what they can access. It might save you from learning this lesson the hard way.
